one_line_takeaway: In simulated 20-turn mental-health dialogues, three production LLMs (DeepSeek-Chat, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Grok-3) crossed clinical safety boundaries (mainly absolute reassurance, professional-role substitution, and relational-dependence claims) in roughly 87-88% of conversations regardless of pressure style, but an adaptive, response-conditioned probing strategy cut the average time-to-first-breach from 9.21 turns to 4.64 turns, showing that single-turn or static-script red-teaming substantially understates how quickly boundary erosion can occur under targeted multi-turn pressure.
key_finding: Across 150 static-progression tests (3 models x 50 profiles), boundary violations were common: DeepSeek-Chat 98.0%, Gemini-2.5-Flash 86.0%, Grok-3 78.0%, overall average 87.3%. Average time-to-breach in static progression was 9.21 turns overall (Gemini-2.5-Flash latest at 9.77, DeepSeek-Chat 9.14, Grok-3 8.69). Under adaptive probing, violation rates converged to about 88% overall (little change from static's 87.3%), but time-to-breach dropped sharply to an overall average of 4.64 turns (DeepSeek-Chat 3.64, Grok-3 4.02, Gemini-2.5-Flash 6.25). Bootstrap 95% CIs on the static-vs-adaptive time-to-breach delta excluded zero for all three models: DeepSeek-Chat advanced by 5.50 turns (95% CI [-6.76, -4.20]), Gemini-2.5-Flash by 3.52 turns (95% CI [-5.33, -1.72]), Grok-3 by 4.67 turns (95% CI [-6.00, -3.40]). Certainty/zero-risk reassurance was the dominant failure mode in both regimes (56.5% of violations in static progression, 48.5% in adaptive probing), followed by professional role drift (20.5% of total violations) and relational dependence/exclusivity (12.1%); direct medical advice was rare (<15% of profile categories). Certain clinical profile types (panic disorder) reached 100% absolute-guarantee violations under both strategies; adaptive probing pushed harmful-belief endorsement to 100% in trauma/delusion/paranoia profiles and relational dependence to 100% in substance-use-disorder (SUD) profiles. The paper also reports that breach rates were significantly higher in Chinese-language dialogues than English, though it does not give a specific percentage difference in the extracted text.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a multi-turn stress-testing framework for evaluating 'safety boundary' erosion in LLM-based mental-health support dialogues, distinct from single-turn prohibited-word detection. It introduces (1) 50 fixed, LLM-generated (GPT-5.2) virtual patient (VP) profiles spanning demographics, a psychological vulnerability, and a 20-round escalation plan; (2) two pressure regimes -- static progression (a pre-scripted, non-adaptive escalation path) and adaptive probing (a Planner/Judge-driven regime that adjusts pressure each round based on the system-under-test's prior response); (3) a rule-based, profile-conditioned Judge (also GPT-5.2) that terminates a dialogue and records a violation the first time one of six pre-defined boundary-breach criteria is triggered, yielding a 'time-to-breach' metric alongside breach rate; and (4) an empirical study applying this framework to three production LLMs (DeepSeek-Chat, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Grok-3) across 150 dialogues (50 profiles x 3 models) up to 20 turns each, plus a bilingual (English/Chinese) extension.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model in the jailbreak/prompt-injection sense. The 'adversary' is a simulated vulnerable help-seeking patient (VP), driven by an LLM, whose goal is to induce the system-under-test into making unsafe relational/clinical commitments over a long dialogue -- e.g., absolute/zero-risk guarantees, professional role substitution (acting as a therapist/case manager), relationship exclusivity/dependence claims, validating pathological/delusional beliefs, self-harm-permissive language, or unauthorized direct medical advice -- rather than eliciting classic prohibited-content outputs. The paper explicitly frames this as boundary erosion accumulating through empathetic, seemingly benign responses rather than through single overtly harmful outputs, and notes it deliberately avoids traditional jailbreak/exploit framing (Appendix C.1).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) results come from structured virtual-patient profiles and LLM-driven simulated dialogues, so may deviate from real clinical scenarios and should be read as revealing robustness/failure trajectories rather than an epidemiological risk estimate for real populations; (2) boundary-breach criteria are discretized (binary trigger/no-trigger) for reproducible statistical comparison but cannot support regression analysis of continuous severity, which real clinical risk assessment would need; (3) profile coverage and interaction-pattern coverage are limited, with expansion to more profiles/patterns left as future work; (4) the VP, Judge, and Planner are all instantiated by a single underlying model (GPT-5.2), which is a potential circularity/consistency risk not directly discussed by the authors beyond noting a manual 20-profile audit for Judge consistency; (5) explicit statement that the goal is not to rank model safety but to characterize violation timing/patterns, so cross-model comparisons should not be read as safety rankings.

datasets_used: 50 fixed virtual patient (VP) profiles generated once by GPT-5.2 from a fixed prompt template (Appendix A), reused identically across all three SUTs and both English/Chinese language settings
quantitative_results: Static progression breach rate: DeepSeek-Chat 98.0%, Gemini-2.5-Flash 86.0%, Grok-3 78.0%, overall average 87.3%
Static progression time-to-breach (turns): overall average 9.21; Gemini-2.5-Flash 9.77, DeepSeek-Chat 9.14, Grok-3 8.69
Adaptive probing breach rate: all three SUTs converged to approximately 88% (little change vs static 87.3%)
Adaptive probing time-to-breach (turns): overall average 4.64; DeepSeek-Chat 3.64, Grok-3 4.02, Gemini-2.5-Flash 6.25
Delta in time-to-breach (adaptive minus static) with bootstrap 95% CI: DeepSeek-Chat -5.50 [-6.76, -4.20]; Gemini-2.5-Flash -3.52 [-5.33, -1.72]; Grok-3 -4.67 [-6.00, -3.40] -- all CIs exclude 0

Failure-type distribution across all recorded violations: certainty/zero-risk reassurance 56.5% (static) vs 48.5% (adaptive); professional role drift 20.5% of total violations; relational dependence/exclusivity 12.1% of total violations; direct medical advice under ~15% of profile categories
Panic-disorder VP profiles: 100% absolute/zero-risk guarantee violations under both static and adaptive strategies
Trauma/delusion/paranoia profiles: adaptive probing increased harmful-belief endorsement to 100%
Substance use disorder (SUD) profiles: adaptive probing pushed relational-dependence violations to 100%
Manual audit: authors manually reviewed conversation logs from a random sample of 20 of the 50 VP profiles to check Judge consistency
150 total tests (3 SUTs x 50 profiles) per language condition, each up to 20 conversational turns
